title: Board of Review Meeting
overview: The Village of Kronenwetter Board of Review held its annual maintenance-year meeting on May 13, 2026, primarily focused on organizational matters, the 2026 assessment roll presentation, and preliminary planning for a mandatory 2027 property revaluation. No formal property tax objections were filed or heard. Finance Director John Jacobs and new assessor Justin Servin of Associated Appraisal Consultants presented assessment data showing the village's total assessed value rose modestly from $735.7 million to $740.3 million, while the level of assessment dropped to 66% of full market value — underscoring the urgency of completing a full revaluation before the state-mandated 2028 deadline.

agenda.item: Call to order and pledge of allegiance
Selection of Board of Review chairperson
Approval of July 10, 2025 Board of Review minutes
Filing and summary of 2026 annual assessment report by assessor's office
Discussion of tax-exempt parcels: pipeline property at 2007 Old Highway 51 and Saint Teresa cemetery parcels
2027 revaluation RFP timeline and planning discussion
Review of objections and scheduled hearings (none pending)

discussions.item: Selection of chairperson and vice chairperson
discussions.body: Trustee Lesniak nominated Dan Jolene as chairperson, seconded by Trustee Quail (Coil); approved unanimously by voice vote. Trustee Mishka nominated Craig Mortensen as vice chairperson, seconded by Trustee Stoll; a motion to close nominations was made by Trustee Quail and seconded by Trustee Mishka, passing by voice vote with at least one abstention. Mortensen was confirmed as vice chair.

discussions.item: Approval of July 10, 2025 Board of Review minutes
discussions.body: Trustee Mishka moved to approve the minutes and Trustee Stoll seconded. The motion passed unanimously by voice vote with no discussion.

discussions.item: 2026 annual assessment report and assessor oath
discussions.body: New assessor Justin Servin of Associated Appraisal Consultants administered his oath of office before presenting the 2026 assessment data. The village's total assessed value increased from $735,730,500 (2025 final) to $740,340,200 (2026), a roughly $5 million gain. The level of assessment fell from approximately 69% to 66% of full market value, reflecting a stabilizing real estate market. Finance Director John Jacobs noted the only correction from open book was a $864 refund to Jennifer Feltch at 1861 Mike Lane after confirming no new construction occurred as of January 1, 2025.

discussions.item: Tax-exempt pipeline property at 2007 Old Highway 51 (Magellan/Williams Pipeline)
discussions.body: Jacobs raised concern that a pipeline property was removed from the tax roll in 2019 after generating approximately an $11,000 tax bill, with the village's share being roughly 20%. Servin said he found a note in paper records from the previous assessor citing state statutes as justification but could not recall the specifics without reviewing the files; he offered to research it during the meeting recess. Jolene recalled a contamination event around 2001 prompting area sewer installation and suggested the property may have served a public entity, which could have triggered exemption. The board agreed to research the paper trail and state statute justification, and Servin suggested that if the exemption is unwarranted, he could assess the property in 2027 and require the owner to re-file exemption paperwork with full documentation by March 1.

Jacobs presented a map showing three parcels that moved to exempt in 2019: the cemetery parcel and two southern parcels totaling approximately 37.38 and 26.33 acres respectively. Servin noted Wisconsin law allows a religious entity only 30 additional acres of exemption beyond the actual cemetery, and the combined acreage of the southern parcels alone exceeds that limit. Servin said he would need to research county records to find documentation. He referenced a similar case in the Town of Hayward where he identified 600 exempt acres affiliated with a hospital and added $24 million to the assessment roll. He noted he can only go back two years to collect omitted taxes (2025 and 2026 in this case).

Jacobs outlined a detailed timeline: issue RFP in late May 2026, board approval in early June, contract start July 1, 2026 with a five-year maintenance contract plus revaluation component. Physical exterior walkarounds of all properties would occur through spring/summer 2027, with the state's equalized value released August 15, 2027 used to finalize numbers. Assessment notices would go out after Labor Day 2027, with open book and Board of Review to follow before the October 15, 2027 submission deadline. Servin confirmed the exterior inspection approach using field teams with iPads, averaging 35-40 properties per person per day, with no drone use. Jacobs emphasized that the revaluation must be completed before 2028 or the state will impose its own revaluation at two to three times the cost.

Board Clerk Jennifer Poyer and Servin confirmed no written objections or formal contest filings were received for the 2026 assessment year. The assessment has been open for review since April 24, 2026. No hearings were scheduled and no subpoenas were requested. Servin explained the standard process: burden of proof rests on the property owner to disprove the assessor's valuation with documentation such as appraisals, cost invoices, or comparable sales.

publicComment: No public comment was offered.
actionItems: Assessor Justin Servin to review paper records and state statute citations from the previous assessor regarding the pipeline property exemption granted in 2019
Servin to research county records for documentation supporting the Saint Teresa church parcel exemptions, particularly for the two southern parcels exceeding the 30-acre religious exemption limit
Finance Director Jacobs to issue draft RFP for reassessment/revaluation services to APC by the following Tuesday, with village board approval targeted for early June 2026
Revaluation contract to begin July 1, 2026 with exterior walkarounds of all village properties conducted through spring/summer 2027
Jacobs and Poyer to develop a public communications plan to explain the revaluation process and its tax implications to residents before assessment notices go out

Board authorized Servin to identify omitted structures during the revaluation walkarounds, particularly those built during the permit gap years (approx. 2022-2023), and assess them as omitted for 2027 going back up to two years
Staff to map and review all 880-class (other tax-exempt) parcels totaling approximately 577 acres to verify current exemption status ahead of the 2027 reassessment

title: Village Board Meeting
overview: The Kronenwetter Village Board voted unanimously 6-0 to approve a Quit Claim Deed transferring village property interests to Marathon County for a proposed Highway Shop Facility, a decision that drew sharp public criticism from a former trustee who argued the land was being given away before required zoning approvals were in place. The board also approved a model e-bike ordinance and a transportation funding resolution, both 6-0. Following a closed session on staff evaluations and compensation, the board approved staff recommendations 5-0 with Trustee Coyle abstaining.

agenda.item: Consent Agenda - Meeting Minutes (April 27 and April 30, 2026)
Vouchers, ACH Transactions, and Credit Card Activity Transactions - Month of March 2026
Quit Claim Deed Proposed Marathon County Highway Shop Facility (CLIPP)
Wausau Metropolitan Planning Organization Draft Model E-Bike Ordinance (CLIPP)

discussions.body: The consent agenda, including minutes from the April 27 and April 30, 2026 Village Board meetings, was approved on a motion by Lesniak, seconded by Myszka. The motion carried by voice vote 6-0.

Jacobs and Administrator Davel answered board questions regarding a bill for a lift station pump out. Trustee Stowell moved, Trustee Myszka seconded, and the motion carried by roll call vote 6-0.

discussions.item: Quit Claim Deed Proposed Marathon County Highway Shop Facility (CLIPP)
discussions.body: Trustee Myszka presented background on the proposal to transfer village property interests to Marathon County via Quit Claim Deed for a proposed Highway Shop Facility. County officials attended and answered board questions about plans for the land. Board members discussed the concept of being a 'good neighbor' to the county, the possibility that the county shop could spur a future interchange on Kowalski Road, and the disposition of a strip of land involved in the transfer. Trustee Coyle moved, Trustee Myszka seconded, and the motion carried by roll call vote 6-0.

discussions.item: Wausau Metropolitan Planning Organization Draft Model E-Bike Ordinance (CLIPP)
discussions.body: Trustee Myszka presented background on the draft model e-bike ordinance developed by the Wausau Metropolitan Planning Organization. Lt. Smart noted the police department had seen no issues with e-bikes to date. Discussion touched on overlapping ordinances in nearby communities, an influx of children riding e-bikes, and potential future safety concerns. Trustee Mortensen moved, Trustee Coyle seconded, and the motion carried by voice vote 6-0.
Administrator Davel presented information on Resolution 2026-007 Transportation Funding Solution. The minutes do not record a named mover or seconder. The motion carried by roll call vote 6-0.

discussions.item: Closed Session - Staff Annual Evaluation and/or Pay Increases
discussions.body: Trustee Lesniak moved, Trustee Myszka seconded, to convene into closed session pursuant to Wis. Stat. 19.85(1)(c) for consideration of employment, promotion, compensation, or performance evaluation data of public employees — specifically staff annual evaluations and/or pay increases. The board entered closed session at 6:38 p.m. and reconvened in open session at 7:32 p.m. on a motion by Myszka, seconded by Coyle, carried 6-0 by roll call vote.

Following the closed session on staff evaluations and compensation, Trustee Myszka moved, Trustee Stowell seconded, to accept the board's recommendations and direct the administrator to act on them. The motion carried by roll call vote 5-0-1, with Trustee Coyle abstaining.

publicComment: Ken Charneski, 2604 16th Road, Kronenwetter, submitted a written comment that was attached to the meeting minutes; his remarks were a lengthy, critical statement opposing the Quit Claim Deed for the Marathon County Highway Shop Facility, accusing Administrator Davel and President Joling of giving away village property without adequate consideration for taxpayer interests, alleging procedural violations in bypassing the APC, and questioning the value of the arrangement given the facility's estimated worth of $50-63 million. Bernie Kramer, 2150 E. State Hwy. 153, Peplin, also addressed agenda item 8I, stating the board should consider David Baker's prior public comment from the CLIPP Committee meeting and arguing that the land should not be given away for a project that has not yet received required approvals.

title: Regular Meeting
overview: The Wausau School Board held its regular May 2026 meeting, approving several key items including the 2026-27 budget reconciliation plan, a 2.63% wage adjustment for non-teacher employee groups, teacher contract renewals, and a 5-year charter contract renewal for the Wausau Area Montessori Charter School. The meeting also featured a student presentation from Maine Elementary fifth graders and concluded with a unanimous vote to move into closed session for nonrenewal and contract evaluation matters.

agenda.item: Call to Order and Roll Call
Pledge of Allegiance and Mission Statement
Maine Elementary Student Presentation - Principal Laura Dred and fifth graders
Consent Agenda - appointments, personnel actions, minutes, bills, donations
2.63% Wage Adjustment for Non-Teacher Employee Groups

discussions.body: Principal Laura Dred introduced four fifth graders — Jaden Dura, Clay Mishka, Adeline Rembrick, and Hudson — who presented on their favorite Maine Elementary events including dress-up days, food fundraisers, Blue and Gold Day, water day, the school forest overnight trip, and the PTO's contributions to the school. Board members responded warmly, joking about adding a dunk tank or 100-foot slide to water day festivities targeting new board members or the superintendent. One board member specifically thanked Principal Dred for her leadership.

The consent agenda covering staff appointments, replacements, contract changes, separations, retirements, meeting minutes from April 13, 27, and 30, payment of bills, budget status, investment report, and donations from organizations including Horsman Neighbors, American Online Giving Foundation, Forest Park Neighborhood, Charles Berger, Josh Dwey, and M3 Insurance was brought to a vote. The motion passed unanimously with no dissent or abstentions noted.

The budget reconciliation plan, previously presented by Assistant Superintendent of Operations Elizabeth Channel at the April Committee of the Whole, was brought for board approval. The motion was made and seconded, and the plan was approved with one abstention noted by the board president. No substantive debate was recorded.

Tabitha Gundram presented the list of teacher contracts for renewal for the 2026-27 school year, noting that state statute requires contracts to be issued by the 15th. The motion passed with one abstention. No opposition or debate was recorded.

discussions.item: 2.63% Wage Adjustment for Non-Teacher Employee Groups
discussions.body: Administration recommended a 2.63% wage adjustment for all employee groups except teachers, whose adjustment will be addressed separately through the negotiation process. Elizabeth Channel noted the item was called out for separate board action even though it was included in the budget reconciliation plan already approved. The motion passed; no vote count beyond 'motion carries' was stated, and no opposition was noted.

discussions.item: Wausau Area Montessori Charter School 5-Year Contract Renewal
discussions.body: Elizabeth Channel presented the WAMCS 5-year charter contract renewal (2026-2031), and Montessori Governance Council President Tyler Berg spoke in person, thanking the board for 20 years of support and expressing hope for another 20 years of partnership. The motion to approve the renewal passed; the board president noted the motion carries with no recorded opposition or abstentions.

Superintendent Bushman requested a motion to enter closed session under Wisconsin statutes 19.85(1)(c) for final notice of nonrenewal and 19.85(1)(e) and (g) for contract evaluation. A roll call vote was taken and all eight members present — Sarah, Charles, John, Pat, Corey, Lance, Yao, and Jim — voted yes. The motion passed 8-0.

publicComment: One public commenter spoke: Dana (last name not given), who identified herself as a WEA member. She reported that teacher appreciation week concluded successfully with staff feeling appreciated by administration and parents, though she noted middle school students were less engaged given the time of year. She also announced a retirement celebration for retiring WEA members scheduled for June 3rd at Bull Falls Brewery.

title: Public Works Committee
overview: The Public Works Committee approved several infrastructure-related items including a state transportation funding resolution, a recycled asphalt crushing contract with King Gravel for $126,900, and construction service proposals for upcoming road projects. The committee also directed staff to develop $100/day fine language for sanitary sewer discharge violations after learning the village treated 15 million gallons of clear water in April due to illegal sump pump connections.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Acknowledge April 2026 water and sewer permits
2025 Weston Water Utility PSC annual report
Water and sewer connection agreement for Radkey Avenue duplex

discussions.body: Michael reported the village received a Local Road Improvement Grant for Birch Street reconstruction between Schofield Avenue and Community Center Drive, a project originally planned for 2029 that will now likely move to 2028. The grant covers about 50% of the estimated $1.5 million project cost. Michael noted the village has received over $9 million in grant funding over the past 4-5 years. Discussion also covered upcoming repairs on Weston Avenue by Integrity Grading and Excavating to address road depressions over sewer trenches, and planned repairs on Schofield Avenue by HOS Construction.

Staff reported on spring activities including road limit removal on April 29th, mailbox and sign repairs, dewatering operations near Weston Avenue tributary for about a week, curb line sweeping, and hot mix patching. Several culverts failed on Jones Street requiring replacement. The committee discussed the ongoing flooding issues near Crane Meadows and the need for a permanent pump solution rather than mobilizing equipment each time.

Josh reported on a service repair at Caribou Coffee, ongoing well maintenance, and significant clear water infiltration issues. April data showed 37 million gallons pumped into the system but 53-54 million gallons treated at the wastewater plant - approximately 15 million gallons of clear water. The Kathleen lift station was identified as having significant issues from sump pump discharges. The committee discussed the upcoming INI study by Inpipe Technologies to identify problem areas.

Michael presented the annual Public Service Commission report showing operating revenues of approximately $3 million and net operating income of $482,911 for 2025. Water loss was 6%. The utility value increased to nearly $34 million with $6.7 million in infrastructure placed in service. The utility has approximately 6,100 customers total. Motion to acknowledge passed unanimously.

Michael explained this League of Wisconsin Municipalities sample resolution supports long-term sustainable state transportation funding. He noted the village has decreased public works budgets recently and compared Wisconsin's automobile ownership costs favorably to neighboring states. Motion to recommend village board adoption passed unanimously.

Michael reported King Gravel submitted the low bid at $4.23/ton for an estimated 30,000 tons totaling $126,900. He calculated the village saves approximately $11/cubic yard compared to City of Wausau costs, resulting in about $130,000 savings across three 2026 projects. Motion to award to King Gravel passed unanimously.

The committee discussed enforcement options for residents illegally discharging sump pumps into the sanitary sewer system. Current code allows fines of $100-$1,000 per day per violation. Trustee Mumper suggested distinguishing between unknowing and willful violations. The committee agreed on $100/day as the starting fine amount. Motion to direct staff to create enforcement language with fines not exceeding $100/day passed unanimously.

Michael requested approval for a temporary lateral connection for a new duplex at 2230 Radkey Avenue until the street is reconstructed. The committee added language requiring Radkey Avenue reconstruction no later than 2029. Motion to recommend village board approval with the 2029 deadline passed unanimously.

The committee approved an intergovernmental agreement with Village of Rothschild for the Military Road project. Weston's utility costs are estimated at $94,860 ($6,156 for sanitary sewer, $88,704 for water main). Steam Construction is the contractor. Motion to recommend village board approval passed unanimously.

Michael presented proposals for construction inspection services. For the Alderson/Jelinek roundabout project, MSA proposed $37,315 versus Clark Dietz at $48,910. For Bladel Avenue, MSA proposed $70,400 versus Clark Dietz at $73,730. Michael recommended MSA for the roundabout and Clark Dietz for Bladel due to having full-time experienced staff rather than a summer intern. Motion to approve both recommendations passed unanimously.

publicComment: No public comment was offered.
actionItems: Staff to create enforcement language for sanitary sewer discharge violations with $100/day fine
Move Birch Street reconstruction project from 2029 to 2028 due to grant funding
Staff to provide cost analysis of clear water treatment at next meeting
Add lighting improvements to Schofield Avenue project scope when scheduled (approximately 2030)
Special assessment meeting for Bladel Avenue scheduled for Thursday, May 28, 2026 at 6:00 PM
Water main flushing to begin next week with notices going out to residents
Modify Radkey Avenue agreement to include 2029 reconstruction deadline
Norcon to begin concrete repairs on Schofield Avenue eastbound lanes starting Wednesday/Thursday

title: Plan Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Plan Commission approved the site plan for the new Mountain Bay Metropolitan Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive, and adopted a new salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection areas. Both items passed unanimously with the commission taking steps to protect municipal water supplies and advance the police facility project.

agenda.item: Call to order and roll call
Site plan review - Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive
Unfinished business - Creation of salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection district
April 2026 staff approved certified survey maps and site plans

discussions.item: Site plan review - Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility
discussions.body: The commission reviewed the site plan for the new police department and municipal court at 3600 Community Center Drive. Staff confirmed the project meets all B2 zoning requirements, setbacks, and building aesthetics. Chief Jeremy explained the building was rotated to accommodate a gas line easement, with the parking lot now positioned over the 36-inch deep pipeline (acceptable to ANR gas company). The building has capacity for 50-60 officers for future growth. Discussion included the alternate storage shed placement and potential for northern expansion. A motion to recommend approval to the board of trustees with staff conditions 2-5 passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection district
discussions.body: Staff presented a program for twice-annual inspections of salt storage facilities within wellhead protection areas - a warm weather inspection focusing on structural integrity and a winter operational inspection. The program includes a standardized inspection worksheet similar to DOT standards. Staff noted one existing facility near Sternberg Road has never gone through the wellhead protection conditional use process and commissioners agreed to reach out to bring it into compliance. The motion to approve the program with reporting to both Plan Commission and Public Works Committee passed unanimously.
Motion to approve the April 13, 2026 meeting minutes as presented passed unanimously.

discussions.body: Approved unanimously without discussion.
Approved unanimously without discussion.

discussions.body: Staff announced the first joint Plan Commission and Village Board steering committee meeting for the comprehensive plan is scheduled for Monday, June 1st at 5:00 p.m. Staff will send email notification and calendar invites.

publicComment: Jim Pensel of 5002 Arrow Street, Weston, spoke via Zoom. He asked the commission to use common sense on landscaping requirements for the new police department to avoid excessive taxpayer costs, referencing the Green Heck Turner building's landscaping standards. He also requested that the salt shed inspection program include annual reporting on site visits and compliance outcomes.

title: Health & Human Services Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Health & Human Services Committee held an educational orientation meeting for new and returning board members, featuring presentations from department heads on the Health Department, Social Services, Veteran Services, and Aging Disability Resource Center. No action items were voted on except approval of the April 1st meeting minutes, which passed unanimously.

agenda.item: Call to order and remote participation confirmation
Approval of April 1st committee meeting minutes
Operational functions required by statute - overview of agenda structure
Review of standing committee ordinance section 2.04, missions, duties and responsibilities
Health Department presentation by Health Officer Caitlyn Suginaka
Social Services Department overview by Administrator Leonard
Veteran Service Department presentation by Aaron Galindo
Aging Disability Resource Center presentation by Executive Director Mike Ray
Next meeting date confirmation and adjournment

discussions.body: Supervisor Cavlli made a motion to approve the minutes, seconded by Vice Chair Rosenberg. The motion carried unanimously with all members voting in favor.

Health Officer Caitlyn Suginaka presented the department's mission of preventing disease, promoting health, and protecting against environmental hazards. She highlighted over 1,200 licenses overseen, the new homeless system response position funded by the county board, public health vending machines providing free tick kits and harm reduction supplies, and the Nurse Family Partnership program which just graduated its first cohort of two-year-olds. Administrator Leonard noted the department has about 37.6 FTE and roughly half of their $5 million budget comes from tax levy, with significant funding from grants and public charges for services.

Administrator Leonard presented on behalf of Director Christa who was unavailable. He described the department's work in child welfare, youth justice, children's long-term support, economic support, and child support. The department has approximately 140 FTE and a 2026 budget of over $22 million with about $7 million from tax levy. Leonard highlighted the successful Family Keys housing stability pilot program, noting many clients served had never had a bank account, credit account, or been on a lease before.

Veteran Service Officer Aaron Galindo reported his 'small but mighty' team of 3.0 FTE had 8,149 interactions in 2025, up from approximately 5,900 in 2024. He noted $44 million in annual VA benefits flowing to Marathon County veterans, plus $2.6 million in retroactive payments in 2025. Galindo discussed suicide prevention efforts following 30 suicides in Marathon County in 2025 (7 were veterans), new housing stability partnerships with Center for Veterans Issues, and the PACT Act's impact on expanding presumptive conditions for VA benefits. Supervisor Kremes asked about regional coordination; Galindo explained state statute requires each county to have a VSO and described good working relationships with neighboring counties.

Executive Director Mike Ray presented on the four-county regional organization serving Marathon, Lincoln, Langlade, and Wood counties with about 60 FTE and 385-400 volunteers. He explained the ADRC serves as the one-stop shop for aging and disability resources and entry point for programs like Family Care and IRIS. Ray highlighted significant financial challenges in the nutrition/Meals on Wheels program, noting federal funding has remained flat since 2024 while food costs increased 40-45% over four years, forcing reductions in meals served, service days, and staffing. Marathon County's levy contribution is $413,135.

publicComment: No public comment was offered.
actionItems: Minutes from April 1st meeting approved unanimously
North Central Healthcare to be scheduled for a separate full presentation before the committee
Mike Ray (ADRC) to present annual report with 2025 data to full board
Committee members to email Chair Boots and Administrator Leonard with agenda items for future meetings
Strategic plan work to be brought to future meetings by Chair Boots

title: Utility Committee Meeting
overview: The Kronenwetter Village Utility Committee met on May 5, 2026, addressing a major flooding crisis that pushed daily sewage flow from a normal 345,000 gallons to 1.1 million gallons, requiring $29,000 in emergency pumping services. The committee approved a ductless mini-split HVAC system for well houses, received updates on the Lift Station 8 construction project, and discussed upcoming development proposals that will require utility planning decisions.

agenda.item: Call to Order and Pledge of Allegiance
UC Duties and Responsibilities - Presentation by Village Administrator Mark
Utility Director Greg's Report - Flooding Emergency, Country Pumpers Response, and Emergency Pump Purchase
Ductless Mini-Split System Purchase for Well Houses
Future Agenda Items / Glacier Meadows Development Discussion

discussions.body: A motion was made and seconded to approve the minutes from the April meeting. The motion carried unanimously with no opposition or discussion.

discussions.item: UC Duties and Responsibilities - Village Administrator Presentation
discussions.body: Village Administrator Mark addressed the committee, emphasizing the need for better communication between committees and the village board on priorities, noting the village struggles with limited funding and lacks a clear unified vision. He highlighted the need for system standardization, particularly regarding pump inventory, noting the village had no spare pump for Lift Station 4. He also announced the recent departure of the community development director and previewed upcoming development proposals including one-acre lot subdivisions with private septics and townhome/zero lot line retirement-style communities, including a developer who paid $1.8 million for the old airfield. Committee member Jim Buck raised concerns about stormwater drainage, calling it a major unresolved issue and noting the lack of any enforceable drainage plan dating back to when sewer and water were installed in 1996.

discussions.item: Utility Director Report - Flooding Emergency and Emergency Pump Purchase
discussions.body: Utility Director Greg reported that recent flooding pushed daily system flow from a normal average of 345,000 gallons to a peak of 1.1 million gallons, primarily through Lift Station 4 which receives flow from Lift Stations 8 and 11. Country Pumpers was contracted for 10 days at a cost of $29,000, deploying two trucks simultaneously. Greg also reported that Village Administrator Jim authorized an emergency purchase of a replacement pump for Lift Station 4 at approximately $8,600-$8,700 to guard against pump failure under extreme strain, noting it arrived or was expected to arrive that day. The committee acknowledged this cost was within the administrator's approval authority. Greg noted Red Marble Metro billing will likely be one and a half to two times higher than projected due to elevated flows, and Finance Director John will present a financial update next month. Greg reiterated his ongoing push to purchase a vac truck and build a garage, noting the RFP for the garage design-build contract is due back May 20th.

discussions.item: Ductless Mini-Split System Purchase for Well Houses
discussions.body: Trustee Alex Fedvik had requested additional information at the prior meeting; Greg provided a vendor sheet and confirmed WPS approved the service connection. The system was budgeted in the adopted November budget. A motion was made to approve the contract to purchase and install the ductless heat pump for Well Numbers 1 and 2 at a cost not to exceed $9,560, with Melbourne as the contractor. The motion was seconded and passed unanimously.

Greg reported the contractor is currently dewatering and preparing to dig the lift station pit, with pipe already run along Corona Motor Drive and some remaining work under the interstate near Kwasky. Completion is targeted for July. A significant concern was raised: the contractor cut power to Lift Station 8, requiring the village to station one of its two mobile generators there, which Greg said could be tied up for a month. Greg stated he told the contractor to obtain a rental generator from United Rental by end of week or the village would rent one and charge it back. Greg also challenged the engineering firm RPS on delays attributed to WPS, questioning when they actually contacted WPS given the contract was signed over a year ago.

discussions.item: Glacier Meadows Development - One-Acre Lots with Private Septics
discussions.body: In response to a question from committee member Jim Buck, Greg clarified that the Glacier Meadows developer informally asked whether the village would be open to one-acre lots with individual private septic systems rather than connecting to the municipal sewer system. No formal proposal or decision was made. Greg confirmed DNR involvement and percolation testing would be required before any approval, and the committee expressed openness to exploring the idea if testing supports it. Greg noted activity was observed at the site with a backhoe and trucks that day.

publicComment: No public comment was offered. The chair noted no one was present for public comment and no emails had been received.
actionItems: Approve and proceed with ductless mini-split installation at Well Houses 1 and 2 with Melbourne, not to exceed $9,560
Emergency pump for Lift Station 4 ($8,600-$8,700) already purchased under administrator authorization; pump to be installed after flows return to normal and existing pumps are inspected
Greg to require Lift Station 8 contractor to provide a rental generator by end of week or village will rent one and bill contractor
Finance Director John to present utility financial report at June meeting to assess budget impact from flooding-related costs and elevated Red Marble Metro billing
Continue pursuing design-build garage RFP with bids due May 20th to eventually house vac truck
Village Administrator to follow up with potential interim zoning/planning consultant to fill gap left by departed community development director; Marathon County offered interim assistance
Greg to meet with Glacier Meadows developer the following week to discuss one-acre lot/private septic proposal; percolation testing and DNR review required before any decision

title: Environmental Resources Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Environmental Resources Committee approved two rezoning petitions and voted to forward a modified fee waiver resolution to the county board that will only waive reconnect fees for septic systems damaged by the April 17, 2026 tornado. The committee also received educational presentations from the Solid Waste, Parks Recreation and Forestry, and Conservation Planning and Zoning departments.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Public hearing: Tim Veland rezoning petition (Green Valley)
Committee deliberation and vote on Green Valley rezoning
Public hearing: Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition (McMillan)
Consideration of fee waiver for properties impacted by April 17, 2026 severe weather
Educational presentation: Solid Waste Department overview
Educational presentation: Parks, Recreation and Forestry Department overview
Educational presentation: Conservation Planning and Zoning Department overview

discussions.body: Supervisor Sefeld made a motion to approve the minutes, seconded by Supervisor Drebeck. The motion carried with no opposition.

discussions.item: Tim Veland rezoning petition - Green Valley
discussions.body: Garrett Pogle presented the staff report for rezoning from urban residential and general agriculture to rural residential in the town of Green Valley. The town of Green Valley recommended approval on March 10, 2026. No public testimony was offered for or against. Supervisor Drebeck moved to approve based on findings of fact, conclusions of law, and town approval. Supervisor Kurt seconded. Motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition - McMillan
discussions.body: The petition sought to rezone from Conservancy Recreation to General Agriculture in the town of McMillan. Supervisor Leur asked about the conservancy nature of the property. Staff explained the property has a home and horse riding stable, and the rezone would make these conforming uses. The town of McMillan recommended approval on April 20, 2026. Supervisor Sefelt moved to approve, seconded by Supervisor Drew. Motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Fee waiver for April 17, 2026 severe weather impacted properties
discussions.body: Director Lori Mskimmons presented a proposal to waive county zoning and septic system fees for properties damaged by the April 17 tornado affecting Ringle, Kronenwetter, Easton, and Weston. Supervisor Drew questioned where to draw the line for future events. Supervisor Leur asked about budget impacts. Supervisor Drebeck expressed concerns about fairness to property owners not in declared disaster areas. After discussion, Supervisor Sefelt moved to forward the resolution to the county board. Supervisor Drebeck offered an amendment to limit the waiver to only reconnect fees ($160) for septic systems, excluding all other fees. The amendment was seconded by Supervisor Kurt and passed. The amended motion to forward to the county board passed but was noted as not unanimous.

Director Dave Hagen Busher presented an overview of solid waste operations, explaining the landfill's 45-year history, the renewable natural gas partnership with Vidi Energy generating royalties for the county, and upcoming capital projects including an $6 million expansion and leachate treatment facility required by 2032 under new state law. He noted lithium-ion battery fires occur about once weekly.

discussions.item: Parks, Recreation and Forestry Department presentation
discussions.body: Tom Lovelin, County Forest Administrator, presented on behalf of Director Jamie. He explained the department operates both county and city park systems under contract, manages 30,700 acres of county forest, and noted Nine Mile County Forest generates $3.25 million in economic impact from out-of-town visitors. Supervisor Sefelt acknowledged Tom's retirement effective June 1st after nearly 30 years of service.

publicComment: No public comment was offered.
actionItems: Forward amended resolution to county board waiving only reconnect fees for septic systems on properties impacted by April 17, 2026 severe weather
Green Valley rezoning petition forwarded to county board for approval
McMillan rezoning petition forwarded to county board for approval
Staff to track costs and requests related to any fee waivers implemented
Joe Tucker selected as new County Forest Administrator following Tom Lovelin's retirement

title: Community Life and Public Safety
overview: The Village of Weston Community Life and Public Safety Committee approved amendments to the nuisance ordinance, recommended an e-bike ordinance with emoto restrictions, and authorized demolition of an abandoned structure at 4104 Shore Avenue. The committee also discussed overnight parking regulations and a crosswalk request, deferring decisions on both to gather more information.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Approval of minutes from previous meeting
Acknowledgment of department reports (Code Enforcement, Fire/EMS, Police)
Mobile food vendors fire inspection requirements

discussions.body: The committee acknowledged reports from Code Enforcement, Fire/EMS, and Police departments. Roman reported that 3020 Mountain View Avenue will be removed from the code enforcement roll after the property sale was completed and the new owner submitted repair permits. Chief Hunt reported the police department had 2,394 calls in March (77 per day), 410 traffic stops, and 281 citations issued. The department has hired its 40th officer. Motion to acknowledge reports passed unanimously.

The committee reviewed final amendments to Chapter 50, including previously discussed changes to tree limb height requirements over streets and walkways (14 and 10 feet). Trustee Olsen made a motion to recommend approval to the Board of Trustees, seconded by Campkey. Motion passed unanimously.

The committee discussed the proposed e-bike ordinance at length, with Chief Hunt strongly advocating for prohibiting emotos (electric dirt bikes) on roadways, sidewalks, and trails due to safety concerns. Committee member Mike Compkey raised concerns about electric scooters used by children. The committee agreed to add language prohibiting emotos from roadways. Trustee Olsen moved to recommend approval with the emoto prohibition on roadways, sidewalks, trails, and public property. Motion seconded by Chwanka and passed unanimously.

Trustee Olsen presented research showing Weston's 12-hour overnight parking window (4 AM to 4 PM) is significantly longer than surrounding municipalities, which typically have 4-5 hour windows. Michael from DPW suggested eliminating the even-odd system and instead signing 6-8 specific problem areas. Chief Hunt preferred keeping overnight parking restrictions to give officers time to clear vehicles before plowing. The committee deferred action, directing Michael to identify specific exception areas to bring back in July.

Mount Olive Church requested a crosswalk between their property and DC Everest parking lot. Staff estimated a basic crosswalk would cost about $2,000, while a lighted system would cost $25,000-$30,000. Chief Hunt had no safety concerns. The committee was reluctant to set precedent of paying for requested crosswalks. Trustee Olsen moved that if Mount Olive is willing to cover the installation cost, the committee recommends board approval. Motion seconded by Chwanka and passed unanimously.

Fire Chief presented a regional fire inspection program for food trucks involving Marshfield, Stevens Point, Wisconsin Rapids, Plover, Rib Mountain, and potentially Weston. One inspection would be valid across all participating municipalities. Chief noted a recent inspection found a food truck with unsafe galvanized LP piping and improperly secured propane cylinders. Chwanka moved to approve Ordinance 26-8, seconded by Campkey. Motion passed unanimously.

Staff presented three address corrections needed for E911 compliance: 5915 Delicoski Street (duplex needing two addresses), 3913 Ross Avenue (sequence correction and secondary unit), and 2211 Radkey Avenue (new duplex on private drive). Chief Hunt mentioned an additional addressing issue at Weston Villa Apartments on Delicoski with eight separate addresses for one structure. Motion to recommend approval passed unanimously.

Staff requested authorization to begin raze order proceedings on an abandoned structure at 4104 Shore Avenue, abandoned since approximately 2010-2014. A previous fire department training burn was deemed unsafe due to the structure's condition. The property owners are current on taxes and have agreed to the demolition approach, with costs to be placed on the tax roll. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Campkey, passed unanimously.

publicComment: Jim Senalt spoke regarding several agenda items. He criticized the new farmers market location at the park, predicting grass damage from vehicle parking and questioning whether parking on grass violates village code. He raised concerns about ADA accessible parking being placed on grass rather than asphalt, noting difficulties for wheelchair users on wet sandy soil. He questioned the origin of new fire inspection requirements for food trucks and raised concerns about mobile food vendor regulations on public property. He also criticized the age of some code enforcement cases, suggesting the committee should receive progress updates on long-standing violations.

overview: The Kronenwetter CLIPP Committee voted 5-0 to support a Quit Claim Deed transferring village-owned property to Marathon County for the County Highway Shop Facility project, clearing the way for a Village Board recommendation on the significant land transfer. The committee also voted 5-0 to support the Wausau Metropolitan Planning Organization's draft model e-bike ordinance, moving toward regional uniformity on e-bike regulations. Road rehabilitation bids for the Flanner/Jamroz project were tabled after Public Works Director Greg Ulman reported bids came in far higher than expected.

Community Development, Planning and Zoning Director Report
Wausau Metropolitan Planning Organization Draft Model E-Bike Ordinance (Old Business)
Discussion of Possible Truck Route on Kowalski Road (Old Business)
Quit Claim Deed - Proposed Marathon County Highway Shop Facility (Old Business)
Consideration of Items for Future Agenda

discussions.item: Selection of a Chairperson and Vice-Chairperson
discussions.body: Trustee Aaron Myszka was nominated for chairperson by Patty Tikalsky, seconded by President Dan Joling, and elected by acclamation. Garrett Lysne was nominated for vice-chairperson by President Joling, seconded by Tikalsky, and approved by voice vote 4-0-1, with Lysne abstaining.

discussions.item: Approval of April 6, 2026 CLIPP Committee Meeting Minutes
discussions.body: Motion by Joling, seconded by Mijal, to approve the April 6, 2026 meeting minutes. Motion carried by voice vote 5-0.
Police Chief Terry McHugh presented his report and answered a committee question regarding the recent hiring of new police officers. No action was taken.

Fire Chief Theresa O'Brien reported that April 2026 was the busiest month she had experienced, with 24 fire calls and 55 medical calls. She also noted that live burn training was a success for her department. No action was taken.

Public Works Director Greg Ulman updated the committee on the effects of recent flooding and tornado events. He reported meeting with Lee Recreation to develop a design proposal for Buska Park and answered committee questions on the cost of Country Pumpers, sewer vacuum truck capability, and park donations. Finance Director John Jacobs and Administrator James Davel also answered questions regarding the budget and the Bulk Item Drop-off event. No action was taken.

discussions.item: Community Development, Planning and Zoning Director Report
discussions.body: Administrator Davel presented the report in the absence of a director, informing the committee that Peter Wegner is no longer with the Village and that the Village is actively hiring a new Community Development Director and planning technician. No action was taken.
Administrator Davel presented the complaint log report, noting that complaint numbers have increased. No action was taken.

discussions.item: Wausau Metropolitan Planning Organization Draft Model E-Bike Ordinance
discussions.body: Motion by Joling, seconded by Mijal, to support the language of the Wausau Metropolitan Planning Organization Draft Model E-Bike Ordinance. Administrator Davel summarized resident comments regarding e-bikes in the village, and Police Chief McHugh stated he had no concerns about the ordinance language. The committee agreed it represented an agreeable and regionally uniform set of standards. Motion carried by voice vote 5-0.

discussions.item: Discussion of Possible Truck Route on Kowalski Road
discussions.body: Public Works Director Ulman reviewed the topic and advised the committee that there is currently no need for a designated truck route on village roads. He noted the issue could be revisited once the distribution center is completed. No action was taken.

discussions.item: Quit Claim Deed - Proposed Marathon County Highway Shop Facility
discussions.body: Motion by Mijal, seconded by Lysne, to recommend the Village Board approve the Quit Claim Deed for Tax Parcel ID Number 145-2707-023-0994. Administrator Davel presented the item and explained the rationale for approval. Marathon County Administrator Lance Leonhard and other county staff attended to discuss the County Highway Garage Facility project, addressing the project timeline, the need for the quit claim deed, and potential benefits for Kronenwetter residents. Leonhard acknowledged the county would make a better effort to attend Village meetings on the subject. The committee voted to carry the motion; no vote count beyond implied 5-0 was recorded.

Public Works Director Ulman requested the item be tabled, reporting that bids came in significantly higher than expected and that additional information was needed. The item was tabled until the next meeting. No vote count was recorded beyond the tabling action.

publicComment: David Baker of 690 Happy Hollow Road submitted written public comment, which was read into the record by the clerk and attached to the minutes. Baker addressed the Quit Claim Deed item (8L) and the broader planning process for the proposed Marathon County Highway Facility. He urged the committee to make any property transfer contingent on the county agreeing to take jurisdiction of and upgrade Highway Business 51 from Village Way to Kowalski Road to four lanes, arguing that without such improvements the safety and traffic burdens currently affecting county highway workers and Wausau residents would simply be transferred to Kronenwetter residents. Baker also asked that the county consider installing a well on the property to avoid straining the village water system, and noted that newly elected Supervisor/Trustee Coyle serves on the Marathon County Infrastructure Committee. Trustee Patty Tikalsky of 2153 Peach Road offered public comment thanking the Kronenwetter Police Department, Fire Department, and Public Works Department for their response and aid during recent severe weather events.
actionItems: Trustee Aaron Myszka elected CLIPP Committee Chairperson by acclamation.
Garrett Lysne elected CLIPP Committee Vice-Chairperson by voice vote 4-0-1.
April 6, 2026 CLIPP Committee meeting minutes approved 5-0.
Committee voted 5-0 to support the Wausau Metropolitan Planning Organization Draft Model E-Bike Ordinance language.
Committee voted to recommend the Village Board approve the Quit Claim Deed for Tax Parcel ID 145-2707-023-0994 related to the Marathon County Highway Shop Facility.
Kronenwetter Flanner/Jamroz Rehabilitation Bids tabled to next meeting pending additional bid information.
Finance Director John Jacobs to present the Capital Improvement Plan with street information to CLIPP in July.
Village continuing to hire a new Community Development Director and planning technician following Peter Wegner's departure.
Truck route on Kowalski Road to be revisited when the distribution center is completed.
Next CLIPP Committee meeting scheduled for June 1, 2026.

title: Special Village Board Meeting
overview: The Kronenwetter Village Board voted 6-0 to terminate the employment of the Community Development Director for just cause at a special meeting called April 30, 2026. The board convened in closed session for roughly 90 minutes to review a specific personnel matter before returning to open session to take the termination vote. Village President Dan Joling directed that remaining personnel issues would be handled by Village Administrator Davel and that legal staff would manage open records notices related to the matter.

agenda.item: Closed Session - Review of specific personnel matter and address next steps (Wis. Stat. s. 19.85(1)(c) and 19.85(1)(f))
Discussion and possible action on items discussed in closed session

discussions.body: Trustee Coyle moved and Trustee Mortensen seconded a motion to convene into closed session under Wis. Stat. s. 19.85(1)(c) and 19.85(1)(f) to review a specific personnel matter and address next steps. The motion carried 7-0 on a roll call vote. Closed session began at 4:32 p.m. Trustee Sorensen left the meeting around 6:00 p.m. during the closed session.

Upon reconvening in open session, Trustee Lesniak moved and Trustee Myszka seconded a motion to terminate the employment of the Community Development Director for just cause. The motion carried 6-0 on a roll call vote. Village President Joling stated that additional personnel issues would be addressed by Village Administrator Davel, that open records notices would be handled by legal staff, and that he asked board members to sign the letter of termination.

publicComment: No public comment was recorded.
actionItems: Community Development Director terminated for just cause, effective April 30, 2026, by a 6-0 board vote.
Village Administrator Davel directed to address remaining personnel issues arising from the matter.
Legal staff directed to handle open records notices related to the termination.
Village Board members asked by President Joling to sign the letter of termination.

title: Special Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, this special meeting of the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to convene in closed session to hold a pupil expulsion hearing. The board was expected to deliberate privately and potentially take action regarding the student expulsion matter, with the possibility of reconvening in open session for further action if necessary.

agenda.item: Closed Session - Pupil Expulsion Hearing pursuant to s. 19.85(1)(a), (f), and (g), and s. 118.125 of Wisconsin Statutes

discussions.body: The Board of Education was scheduled to convene in closed session under Wisconsin Statutes sections 19.85(1)(a), (f), and (g), as well as section 118.125, to conduct a pupil expulsion hearing. The board was expected to deliberate privately at the conclusion of the hearing and potentially take action in closed session if necessary and appropriate. Following the closed session, the board was scheduled to potentially reconvene into open session for further action.

publicComment: No public comment period was included on this agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to consider action on a pupil expulsion matter during or following the closed session hearing

title: Regular Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Board held its adjourned organizational meeting, approving committee appointments for the new term and electing Supervisors Hapa and Arstead to at-large executive committee positions. The board also unanimously approved a resolution to reduce landfill tipping fees for storm debris following the severe weather emergency declaration, dropping rates from $65/ton to approximately $36.94/ton for affected residents.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance and moment of reflection
Election of at-large executive committee members
Resolution 24-26: Out of home placement reserve increase

discussions.body: Chair Gibbs presented committee appointments developed with Vice Chair Boots, emphasizing goals of increasing diverse perspectives, developing leadership among newer supervisors (34% in first or second term), and honoring supervisor preferences. Motion by Supervisor Cavelli, seconded by Supervisor Crims. The motion carried but was not unanimous, with some technical issues noted where Supervisor Nisa was marked absent but unable to vote.

discussions.item: Election of at-large executive committee members
discussions.body: Three supervisors were nominated: Supervisor Hapa (nominated by Supervisor Mask), Supervisor Leur (nominated by Supervisor Rosenberg), and Supervisor Arstead (nominated by Supervisor Boots). Supervisor Rosenberg spoke in support of Leur, citing her advocacy for children and elders. Paper ballots were distributed and supervisors selected two of the three nominees. Supervisors Hapa and Arstead were elected to the executive committee.
Items C10 through C14A were approved on consent. Motion by Supervisor Cavelli, seconded by Supervisor Drebeck. The motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Resolution 24-26: Out of home placement reserve increase
discussions.body: Resolution from Health and Human Services and HR Finance and Property committees. Motion by Supervisor Morash, seconded by Supervisor Robinson. The motion carried but was not unanimous.
Motion by Supervisor P, seconded by Supervisor Robinson. The motion carried but was not unanimous.

discussions.item: Resolution 26-26: Landfill tipping fee modification for storm debris
discussions.body: Administrator Leonard and Solid Waste Director Hagen Busher presented the proposal from the Solid Waste Management Board to reduce tipping fees for debris from the recent severe weather emergency. The state is waiving $13.10/ton and Ringle is waiving $2.33/ton. The county proposed reducing fees from the standard $65/ton gate rate to approximately $36.94/ton (covering costs only) through June 17th. Two residents, Jessica Toasty and Matt Haybeck, were granted special permission to speak, requesting the county waive fees entirely. Multiple supervisors asked questions about coverage, timelines, and tracking. The board first voted unanimously to suspend rules to consider the resolution directly, then approved Resolution 26-26 unanimously.

publicComment: Two residents were granted special dispensation to speak on the landfill tipping fee resolution. Jessica Toasty requested Marathon County wave fees entirely for six months, noting the state, DNR, and Ringle had waived their portions and that residents are facing significant unreimbursed costs. Matt Haybeck spoke on behalf of approximately 44 affected homeowners, noting many are still waiting for insurance adjusters and requested extended fee relief.
actionItems: Committee appointments approved for the new term
Supervisors Hapa and Arstead elected to at-large executive committee positions
Resolution 26-26 approved reducing landfill tipping fees for storm debris to approximately $36.94/ton through June 17th
Administrator Leonard to continue discussions with the Community Foundation regarding reimbursement mechanisms for unreimbursed insurance costs
Solid Waste Management Board to develop a clear policy on handling similar disaster situations within six months
Supervisors interested in attending Solid Waste Board meetings to be notified of future meetings
